title: GitHub GraphQL API
description: The GitHub GraphQL API enables precise data fetching — request exactly what you need in a single round trip. Unlike REST's waterfall of N+1 requests, GraphQL resolves nested resources in one query tree traversal. This guide covers schema design, mutations, cursor-based pagination, and real-world query patterns.

sections.title: GraphQL vs REST
sections.description:
GraphQL eliminates N+1 requests by allowing nested resource fetching in a single query. Where REST requires GET /users/:user → GET /repos/:owner/:repo → GET /issues/:id, GraphQL resolves query { user { repositories(first:10) { issues(first:10) { title } } } } in one round trip.

Trade-offs: GraphQL responses cannot be cached at the HTTP level (always POST, always 200), responses are larger per query, and the learning curve is steeper. REST is better for simple CRUD, caching, and tooling with standard HTTP semantics.

sections.title: Schema & Queries
sections.description:
The GraphQL schema defines the shape of available data. Root types: Query (read), Mutation (write), Subscription (real-time). Connections use Relay-style pagination with edges { node { ... } } structure.

Explore the schema interactively at https://docs.github.com/en/graphql/overview/explorer or use the built-in GraphQL Explorer. The schema is self-documenting via introspection queries.

sections.title: Mutations
sections.description:
Mutations modify data on GitHub. Every mutation returns the mutated object so you can confirm the result in the same request. Mutations follow the pattern mutation { actionName(input: { ... }) { clientMutationId | field } }.

All mutations require the mutate scope on your token. Common mutations include addComment, updateIssue, mergePullRequest, createRepository, and requestReviews.

sections.title: Cursor Pagination
sections.description:
All list fields in the GraphQL API use cursor-based pagination via the Relay Connection spec. Key arguments: first/last (number of items), after/before (cursor string). The pageInfo object provides hasNextPage, hasPreviousPage, startCursor, and endCursor.

Cursors are opaque, stable strings that don't change even when new items are added to the collection — ideal for real-time data where page-based offsets would shift.

sections.title: Real-World Queries
sections.description:
Real-world patterns include fetching open PRs with review status, checking mergeability before merging, getting issue counts by label, and fetching repository metadata with language breakdowns. Always request only the fields you need to minimize response size and cost.

GraphQL node limits default to 500,000 nodes per query. Fragment composition and aliased queries can help stay within limits while maximizing data fetched.

details:
GitHub's GraphQL API is powered by the /graphql endpoint and accepts POST requests with a JSON body containing query (the GraphQL query string) and optional variables (a JSON object mapping variable names to values).

Query Structure

A query begins with the operation type (query, mutation, or subscription), an optional operation name, and the selection set. Connections are always wrapped in edges { node { ... } } with pageInfo for navigation. Use GraphQL fragments (fragment X on Y) to reuse field sets across queries.

Rate Limiting

GraphQL rate limiting is based on point cost, not request count. Each query is assigned a cost based on the number of nodes traversed, with a default limit of 5000 points per hour for authenticated users. Complex queries are rejected with a 200 response containing an errors array with type RATE_LIMITED.

Aliases & Variables

Aliases let you request the same field with different arguments in a single query. Variables keep queries clean and reusable — define them in the operation signature with $varName: Type and pass values in the variables JSON object.

Code Examples

# Query: open PRs with review status and mergeability
query($owner: String!, $repo: String!, $first: Int!) {
  repository(owner: $owner, name: $repo) {
    pullRequests(first: $first, states: [OPEN]) {
      edges {
        node {
          title
          url
          mergeable
          reviews(last: 1) {
            edges {
              node {
                state
                author { login }
              }
            }
          }
        }
      }
      pageInfo {
        hasNextPage
        endCursor
      }
    }
  }
}

# Mutation: add label and comment to an issue
mutation($issueId: ID!, $labelId: ID!, $comment: String!) {
  addLabelsToLabelable(input: {
    labelableId: $issueId,
    labelIds: [$labelId]
  }) { clientMutationId }
  addComment(input: {
    subjectId: $issueId,
    body: $comment
  }) { commentEdge { node { url } } }
}

# Fragment reuse across queries
fragment RepoFields on Repository {
  nameWithOwner
  description
  primaryLanguage { name }
  stargazerCount
  forkCount
}
